title: A Mid-Infrared Platform Based on Strontium Tweezer Arrays
authors: Aaron Holman, Ximo Sun, Bojeong Seo, Joshua Corn, Zezheng Zhu, Yuan Xu, Jiahao Wu, Nanfang Yu, Dmytro Filin, Marianna Safronova, Sebastian Will
abstract: Subwavelength atomic tweezer arrays, in which atoms can be positioned at distances smaller than their emission wavelength, have been proposed as a versatile platform to study collective emission phenomena, such as superradiance and subradiance. Experimentally, the realization of such arrays has been a challenge as typical emission wavelengths in the visible or near-infrared are short compared to typical tweezer spacings in the micrometer range. Here, we use $^{88}$Sr atoms in optical tweezer arrays to access a mid-infrared transition at 2,923 nm ($5s5p\:^{3}P_{2} \rightarrow\, 5s4d\:^{3}D_{3}$). We identify a magic trapping wavelength at 597.14(3) nm and demonstrate single-atom preparation and imaging with high fidelity. In addition, using 2,923 nm light, we demonstrate resolved-sideband cooling of tweezer-trapped strontium. Beyond enabling studies of collective emission phenomena in flexible arrangements of atoms, our platform opens novel opportunities for dipolar many-body physics and enhanced control over Rydberg dynamics and the strontium fine-structure qubit.
summary: The research establishes a novel platform using strontium atoms trapped in optical tweezer arrays, specifically engineered to access a mid-infrared transition at 2,923 nm. Key achievements include high-fidelity single-atom trapping and demonstrating resolved-sideband cooling in the metastable state. This platform opens new avenues for studying dipolar many-body physics and quantum control beyond traditional lattice setups.
main_problem: To develop a versatile platform using subwavelength atomic tweezer arrays to study collective emission phenomena, such as superradiance and subradiance, by accessing mid-infrared transitions.
main_result: The authors successfully demonstrated single-atom preparation, high-fidelity imaging, and resolved-sideband cooling of trapped strontium atoms using a mid-infrared transition at 2,923 nm.
method: The experiment utilizes optical tweezer arrays generated via holographic metasurfaces to trap strontium atoms, followed by precision spectroscopy and cooling measurements on the mid-IR transition.
model_or_system: The system involves Strontium atoms trapped in optical tweezer arrays, leveraging a mid-infrared transition ($5s5p~^3P_2 ightarrow 5s4d~^3D_3$) at 2,923 nm. The trapping is stabilized using a magic wavelength at 597.14 nm.
key_observables: Single-atom imaging fidelity (99.7%), cooling temperature ($\approx 14 \mu	ext{K}$ radial), and linewidth measurements ($\Gamma/(2\pi) = 74 \pm 10 	ext{ kHz}$).
important_parameters: Mid-IR transition wavelength (2,923 nm), magic trapping wavelength (597.14 nm), and trap frequencies ($\approx 95 	ext{ kHz}$).
main_assumptions: The model for temperature extraction assumes the axial temperature remains constant during radial cooling measurements.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the potential of the 2.9 $\mu$m transition for subwavelength arrays and qubit control; spectroscopy shows level structure and magnetic field effects; and cooling figures compare uncooled vs. cooled recapture probabilities.
paper_structure: The paper details the platform realization using metasurfaces, followed by single-atom preparation and imaging; then, it presents precision spectroscopy of the mid-IR transition; finally, it demonstrates resolved-sideband cooling using time-of-flight measurements.
why_interesting: This work significantly expands the accessible physics regime for trapped atoms by utilizing mid-infrared transitions, enabling studies of dipolar interactions and collective effects in flexible, subwavelength geometries.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Experimental potential of the strontium 2.9 µm transition in atomic tweezer arrays. (a) Optical tweezers, at a magic wave- length for the 2.9 µm transition, allow arrangements of single atoms with programmable geometries. (b) Subwavelength arrays, where the distance d is smaller than the emission wavelength λ, open a path to studying collective emission phenomena. (c) The transition dipole moment of 5.34 Debye can enable new systems with elastic dipole-dipole interactions, realizing effective spin-spin coupling. (d) Coupling of 2.9 µm light to the fine-structure qubit opens novel op- portunities for cooling and quantum state control.

FIG. 3. Single-atom preparation and imaging in 597 nm tweezers. (a) An averaged image of a 5 × 5 atomic tweezer array with 5 µm spacing. (b) Back-to-back imaging analysis for a typical trap, high- lighted with a red box in panel (a). (c) Averaged fluorescence image of one-dimensional chains with λ/2, λ, 3/2λ, 2λ and 5/2λ spacing. (d) Averaged fluorescence image of a ring-shaped array with 36 sites spaced by λ. Each image in (a), (c), and (d) is an average of 100 individual images without parity projection.

FIG. 2. Strontium level structure and trapping wavelength. (a) Level diagram of 88Sr and relevant transitions. The mid-infrared 2,923 nm transition from 3P2 to 3D3 is central to this work. The mJ = ±2 →±3 transitions form closed two-level systems. (b) (left) Under magic trapping conditions, the optical tweezer potentials for atoms in the 3P2 and 3D3 states are identical. (right) Theoretical prediction of the polarizability for the mJ = ±2 →±3 transitions, indicating magic conditions at around 597.5 nm.

FIG. 4. Spectroscopy of the 2.9 µm 3P2 →3D3 transition in a Sr tweezer array at 597 nm. (a) An energy level diagram illustrating the shifts induced by 597 nm light on the Zeeman sublevels of the 3P2 and 3D3 manifolds; levels in the solid gray (hatched gray) are trapped (anti- trapped). The tweezer light is linearly polarized. (b) Loss spectrum of tweezer-trapped atoms following illumination with 2.9 µm light. Here, ∆is defined as the detuning relative to the free-space resonance. Vertical color bars indicate the predicted resonance shifts using the same color scheme as in (a); hatched color bars indicate transitions to anti-trapped levels.

FIG. 5. Precision spectroscopy of the closed |mJ| = 2 →3 transition. (a) In the absence of a magnetic field, a linewidth of Γ/(2π) = 74(10) kHz is measured, obtained from a Lorentzian fit. (b) In the presence of a magnetic field the previously degenerate transitions mJ = ±2 → ±3 split symmetrically. The splitting is about 0.4 MHz. The mag- netic field is about 0.14(1) G and aligned along the polarization vec- tor of the optical tweezers. The linewidth of the left dip and the right dip are Γ/(2π) = 90(15) and 107(20) kHz, respectively.
